posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
One of the joys for me inside the world of wine is to share this passion with others. The consummate sharing experience is when I can treat a winemaking friend with a treasured bottle of his or her`s wine.

Fortunately, I have many opportunities to do just that and the most recent occasion was a couple of nights ago when Burt Williams of Williams Selyem fame and I planned dinner out.

First, we enjoyed a white Burgundy on our rooftop along with my better half and then Burt and I walked a couple of blocks to Opal Restaurant where our friend, co-owner, Richard Yates, had a preferred window table reserved for us.

We were blessed to have the epitome of all wait staff, Shannon, as our waitress/ server and Richard joining us at our table. We ate well and drank a fine Burt made Pinot Noir from my cellar.

2015 PIERRE-YVES COLIN-MOREY LA PUCELLE SAINT AUBIN- from the fantastic 2015 vintage, this jewel complimented the open air, 360 degree, crystal clear, sun setting view of the Santa Barbara area from our rooftop on a blue bird day. The wine was replete with bracing acidity and a major dose of lemon-lime and kiwi notes; once less chilled, more intense flavors unfolded along with a lush, creamy texture that carried all of the goodness to the back end; we love PYCM.

1995 WILLIAMS SELYEM RUSSIAN RIVER VALLEY PINOT NOIR- consistently delivering fresh, spicy strawberry, red raspberry and red cherry notes, this bottle satisfied as all before it have; still youthful and so viable, this 22 year old Pinot shines and gives up a bounty of treasures albeit with grace and elegance.

The twinkle in Burt`s eyes after the first sip was enough for me to delight in another special occasion with an icon and dear friend.

Cheers,
Blake

posts.name: Jerry Hey
posts.cooked: Nice notes and to share the wine that Burt made. I also love PYCM and yes the 2015 vintage, according to Pierre-Yves in my tasting in December, is excellent for him. The Saint Aubin were all very good and the last five I tasted - Meursault Genevrieres and Perrieres, Corton Charlemagne, Batard, and Chevalier were outstanding.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
Jerry Hey:

Nice notes and to share the wine that Burt made. I also love PYCM and yes the 2015 vintage, according to Pierre-Yves in my tasting in December, is excellent for him. The Saint Aubin were all very good and the last five I tasted - Meursault Genevrieres and Perrieres, Corton Charlemagne, Batard, and Chevalier were outstanding.

Jerry, I too got the feedback about the 15s from P-Y at the tasting and took heed by purchasing many. All of the above you mention are truly outstanding.

posts.name: Joshua Kates
posts.cooked:
Good to know about the PYCM 15’s; the 14 of the La Pucelle is one of the finest whites I’ve had from him, really blew my socks off.

Always great to follow your exploits as well, Blake!

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
I’m a long time buyer of PYCM, with over 300 bottles in the cellar, a lot of it Saint Aubin. However, so far I have not been impressed by the 15 PYCM Saint Aubins nor the 15 H. Boillot whites. I’ve tasted thru 8 different wines from both producers, and in general they have come across as fresh on the positive side, but on the negative side they can be loose knit, simple, low acid wines that lack the energy, complexity and cut that I like in WB. The 2015 vintage wines that we’ve sampled so far are:

H. Boillot (CC, PM Mouchere, PM Perrieres, M Genevrieres, M Perrieres)
PYCM (SA En Remilly, SA Chateniere, Santenay La Comme)

I know its still early, but the flavor and acid profiles in the wines remind me of 2005 white burgundy. And if the wines don’t have acidity, tension and energy at this point they are not going to get better with age.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
Joshua Kates:

Good to know about the PYCM 15’s; the 14 of the La Pucelle is one of the finest whites I’ve had from him, really blew my socks off.

Always great to follow your exploits as well, Blake!

I appreciate it Joshua.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
NICE. Blake. Glad to see you posting. I saw Stephen last night and it reminded me of you.
Hope all is well.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
dcornutt:

NICE. Blake. Glad to see you posting. I saw Stephen last night and it reminded me of you.
Hope all is well.

Don, Stephen sent me the menu and a photo of the wines at a recent dinner. Looks like top class is still prevalent in Atlanta. I`m happy to be back in the loop and posting.

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked:
Counselor Seiber and I got out for a round of golf y/day and on the 19th, we shared a few glasses of the 15 PYCM La Pucelle. Gotta say, that is a helluva wine. Blake’s comment about ‘bracing’ acidity is quite true. This wine is like laser beam of energy that really got my attention. Just a shout out that the wine was drinking great, right from the pull of the cork. And I’ll add as well, there were qualities in this PYCM that reminded me of Kutch chardonnay, especially with the flint and core of the wine.

  • 2015 Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey Saint-Aubin La Pucelle - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Saint-Aubin (4/17/2018)
    Lovely stuff. Depth of white pepper, lemon, smoke, flint, light grapefruit. Medium weight with an intensity that creates a long finish. Great stuff.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Subu Ramachandran
posts.cooked: Great notes Blake…PYCM Saint Aubin’s are the only white burgs I bought in '15s. Glad they are showing well!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Subu Ramachandran:

Great notes Blake…PYCM Saint Aubin’s are the only white burgs I bought in '15s. Glad they are showing well!

The lower appellations are showing well, but the more serious wines are shutting down hard. The Bâtard, Genevrieres and Corton-Charlemagne are almost completely inscrutable. I followed open bottles (at room temperature) for a week to review, and if anything the wines got tighter. They saw a healthy dose of SO2 but I think they’re also just inherently built for the long haul. I’ll be cellaring my own bottles for an extended period.

Pierre-Yves says the vintage reminds him of 1985, FWIW.

posts.name: Subu Ramachandran
posts.cooked:
William,

Thanks for the note by Pierre-Yves, I hope the reds do as well as the '85s. For whites, I’m just scaling back on white burgs and chards in general. Too many 14s in cellar and more critically, I’m more keen on other whites these days…

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
Frank Murray III:

Counselor Seiber and I got out for a round of golf y/day and on the 19th, we shared a few glasses of the 15 PYCM La Pucelle. Gotta say, that is a helluva wine. Blake’s comment about ‘bracing’ acidity is quite true. This wine is like laser beam of energy that really got my attention. Just a shout out that the wine was drinking great, right from the pull of the cork. And I’ll add as well, there were qualities in this PYCM that reminded me of Kutch chardonnay, especially with the flint and core of the wine.

  • 2015 Pierre-Yves Colin-Morey Saint-Aubin La Pucelle - France, Burgundy, Côte de Beaune, Saint-Aubin (4/17/2018)
    Lovely stuff. Depth of white pepper, lemon, smoke, flint, light grapefruit. Medium weight with an intensity that creates a long finish. Great stuff.

Posted from CellarTracker

How special, for you two guys to get some time together and open a beauty. How did you remember the post on the PYCM almost a year ago Frank? Looking forward to my mini, one night Mike Hammer visit to OC someone in mid to late May TBD.

posts.name: Frank Murray III
posts.cooked:
Blake, my memory didn’t do it, but the search feature did. “Pucelle” is all it took.

Hope to see you in May.

posts.name: Steve Brickley
posts.cooked: Excellent note Blake. Reflecting on your first comment, events and friends like yours bring wine to a new level of enjoyment. Cheers!

posts.name: C Chen
posts.cooked:
Joshua Kates:

Good to know about the PYCM 15’s; the 14 of the La Pucelle is one of the finest whites I’ve had from him, really blew my socks off.

Completely agree, Joshua. I just realized I haven’t been seeing the La Pucelles anywhere. Need to load up! Thanks for the notes, Blake and Frank.

Feel free to send great prices my way, guys.

posts.name: Blake Brown
posts.cooked:
Steve Brickley:

Excellent note Blake. Reflecting on your first comment, events and friends like yours bring wine to a new level of enjoyment. Cheers!

Much appreciated Steve.
